a: Business Intelligence is the practice of turning raw company data into clear charts, dashboards, and reports that help people make decisions. The goal is to answer questions like 'how are sales doing?' without anyone writing code.
fs: BI turns data into decisions: it makes numbers visible and understandable to non-technical people.
fj: Thinking BI is about collecting data. The hard part is presenting it so a busy person 'gets it' in seconds.

a: A dashboard is a single screen that shows several related charts and numbers together, so you can see the health of something at a glance. Think of a car dashboard: speed, fuel, and temperature all in one view.
fs: A dashboard is a curated set of linked visuals answering one audience's recurring questions in one place.
fj: Cramming every chart you have onto one page. A good dashboard answers a focused question, not all of them.

a: A metric is a number you want to track and total up, like revenue, number of orders, or signups. It is the 'how much?' value that a chart adds up or averages.
fs: A measure is the numeric value you aggregate (sum, average, count); it's the height of the bars.
fj: Confusing a metric with a category. 'Revenue' is a measure; 'Country' is what you slice it by.

a: A dimension is a category you use to break a metric apart, like date, region, product, or customer type. It answers 'by what?' so 'sales by month' uses month as the dimension.
fs: A dimension is a descriptive attribute you group or filter by; it's the labels on the axis.
fj: Treating dates or IDs as numbers to sum. You don't add up months or zip codes; you group by them.

q: What is a filter in BI?
a: A filter narrows the data down to just the part you care about, like only this year, or only one region. It changes what the charts show without changing the underlying data.
fs: A filter restricts the rows feeding every visual, so the whole dashboard answers 'for this slice only'.
fj: Expecting a filter to edit or delete data. It only hides rows from view; the source stays untouched.
